channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: GFN News #189 | How the UK Tobacco Bill Punishes Smokers Trying to Quit?
item.description: The UK’s new Tobacco and Vapes Act is intensifying debate over whether current tobacco control policies adequately support existing smokers attempting to quit. While the legislation aims to create a “smoke-free generation” by phasing out future tobacco sales, critics argue that restrictions on vape advertising, flavours, and alternative nicotine products may unintentionally reduce access to lower-risk options for adult smokers. Researchers and harm reduction advocates continue to warn that policies focused primarily on prohibition and youth prevention risk overlooking the role of non-combustible nicotine products in smoking cessation. The debate reflects broader tensions between public health prevention strategies, consumer behaviour, and evidence-based approaches to reducing smoking-related disease and mortality. (Published on May 25, 2026bygfn.tv)

item.title: Advocates Fear EU “Domino Effect,” as Belgium Bans Vape Flavors
item.description: Belgium’s decision to ban vape flavours is intensifying debate over the role of flavours in smoking cessation and tobacco harm reduction. Supporters of the restrictions argue that flavour bans are necessary to reduce youth appeal and nicotine uptake, while critics warn that such measures may also make vaping less effective or attractive for adult smokers attempting to quit cigarettes. Researchers continue to debate whether flavour restrictions adequately balance youth prevention goals with evidence suggesting that non-tobacco flavours can play an important role in switching away from combustible tobacco. The policy change reflects wider international tensions surrounding nicotine regulation, risk communication, and evidence-based approaches to reducing smoking-related disease and mortality. (Published on May 25, 2026byfiltermag.org)

item.title: WHO and the Cigarette-Shaped Imagination
item.description: Criticism of global tobacco control policy is increasingly focusing on the way smoking continues to shape public health thinking around nicotine. Researchers and harm reduction advocates argue that many regulatory frameworks still approach all nicotine products through the historical lens of combustible cigarettes, despite major differences in toxicological risk. The discussion highlights concerns that outdated assumptions about nicotine may be influencing public perception, media narratives, and policy decisions surrounding reduced-risk alternatives. Questions are increasingly being raised over whether public health institutions are adapting quickly enough to scientific evidence and evolving patterns of nicotine use. (Published on May 25, 2026bydisobedientmargins.com)

item.title: World Vape Day vs World No Tobacco Day: A Growing Divide Over Strategy Despite the Shared Ultimate Goal
item.description: Growing divisions within the global nicotine debate are becoming increasingly visible through competing public health narratives surrounding World Vape Day and World No Tobacco Day. While both movements ultimately focus on reducing smoking-related disease and mortality, major disagreements remain over the role of vaping and other reduced-risk nicotine products in achieving that goal. Harm reduction advocates argue that non-combustible alternatives can accelerate declines in cigarette smoking, whereas opponents continue to emphasise concerns related to youth uptake, nicotine dependence, and long-term uncertainty. The debate reflects broader tensions between abstinence-focused tobacco control strategies and approaches centred on relative risk, consumer behaviour, and evidence-based harm reduction. (Published on May 25, 2026byvapingpost.com)

item.title: Australia abandoned harm minimisation on smoking – and fuelled a black market
item.description: Australia’s increasingly restrictive approach to smoking and nicotine regulation continues to fuel debate over the unintended consequences of prohibition-focused public health policy. Critics argue that limiting access to lower-risk nicotine alternatives while maintaining high tobacco taxes has contributed to the rapid growth of illicit cigarette markets across the country. Researchers and harm reduction advocates continue to warn that policies focused primarily on reducing nicotine use overall may overlook the behavioural realities of addiction and consumer demand. The debate reflects broader international tensions between harm minimisation, public health objectives, taxation policy, and evidence-based approaches to reducing smoking-related disease and mortality. (Published on May 25, 2026bypearlsandirritations.com)
